paper.title: ACTH-dependent Cushing Syndrome with No Peripheral Response But a Marked Petrosal Sinus ACTH Response to Desmopressin

entities.name: ACTH-dependent Cushing Syndrome with No Peripheral Response But a Marked Petrosal Sinus ACTH Response to Desmopressin

evidence_entities.text: Desmopressin is increasingly used for the diagnosis of Cushing disease (CD) since corticotropin-releasing hormone became unavailable.
Intravenous desmopressin administration produced no ACTH or cortisol increase.
Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) with desmopressin stimulation resulted in elevated central-to-peripheral ACTH ratio and prolactin co-secretion, while peripheral ACTH remained stable.

The mechanism by which desmopressin stimulates ACTH secretion in CD may result from overexpression of AVPR1B receptors as well as ectopic AVPR2 receptors in corticotroph adenomas, which is more prevalent in tumors harboring USP8 mutations.
Over the previous 2 years he had developed diabetes, hypertension, hypokalemia, osteoporosis, obesity, and overt Cushingoid appearance.
However, pathology showed normal pituitary tissue with rare Crooke's cells, suggesting cortisol excess.
Two weeks later, the patient was admitted to the emergency unit with nausea, vomiting, and lethargy.
AI was diagnosed, and the patient's condition promptly resolved with IV hydrocortisone and fluid resuscitation.
According to a recent meta-analysis, the desmopressin stimulation test has a sensitivity of 88% for discriminating CD from normal patients with clinical or biochemical features suggestive of CS.

notes: This case report describes an unusual presentation of ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome with discordant peripheral and central responses to desmopressin stimulation.
The patient showed no peripheral ACTH response to desmopressin but had a marked central response during inferior petrosal sinus sampling.
The case highlights the complexity of diagnosing and treating cyclic Cushing syndrome.
